basics.name: Nikolay Ternov
basics.summary: Associate Professor of Sociology and Political Science at L.N. Gumilyov Eurasian National University (ENU), Astana, Kazakhstan. Works in computational social science, digital media, political communication, urban sociology, and post-Soviet studies.

work.name: L.N. Gumilyov Eurasian National University (ENU)
work.position: Associate Professor of Sociology and Political Science
work.summary: Teaching and research in sociology and political science, with a focus on computational social science, digital media, political communication, and post-Soviet/Central Asian studies.

publications.name: Personal Legitimacy in the Structure of Public Perception of Power Among VK Users
publications.summary: Investigates how VK users perceive political legitimacy and authority in digital environments.

publications.name: Digital delegitimization: Biden representations in the astroturfing discourse of the RuNet
publications.summary: Analyzes strategies of digital delegitimization in Russian-language online discourse, focusing on representations of Joe Biden in coordinated astroturfing environments.

publications.name: The sociopolitical January 2022 protests in Kazakhstan’s Telegram channels: Agenda interception)
publications.summary: Examines Telegram channels and political communication during the January 2022 protests in Kazakhstan.

publications.name: Legitimacies Conflict: The Evolution of Perceived Legitimacy in Modern Russia
publications.summary: Explores the evolution of legitimacy narratives in modern Russia and conflicts between institutional and public perceptions of political legitimacy.

publications.name: Emigration to the internet: "Samizdat" and the genesis of contemporary Russian nationalism
publications.summary: Analyzes the historical and ideological role of online samizdat communities in shaping contemporary Russian nationalist discourse.

publications.name: Nationalism and Siberian archeology of the 19th century
publications.summary: Examines the relationship between nationalism, regional identity formation, and archeological discourse in 19th-century Siberia.
